learning_outcomes.description: Know common stages in viral replication cycle and unique replication strategies of different virus families.
Critically review the strategies for creating and applying recombinant virus vectors as tools for treatment and prevention of diseases.
Review how viruses interact with their hosts at the level of the cell, the whole organism and the community in the context of transmission and disease.
Know how to apply knowledge of viral structures for development of vaccines and antiviral agents.
Understand how viruses evolve, and new strains emerge.
Gain specific knowledge of human respiratory viruses, veterinary viruses and viruses infecting insects and plants
Obtain practical skills on how to detect viral infections and analyse virus encoded genes and proteins.
Analyse changes in cell transcriptome induced by viral infections.
Learn about virology career pathways, media engagement and commercialization.

assessments.task_description:
After completing all practical sessions each student will need to submit their individual practical reports via Turnitin as a pdf file.

Further details on how to prepare the practical report will be provided in respective tutorials and on Blackboard.

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI)and Machine Translation (MT)

This assessment task evaluates students' abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT). Students are advised that the use of AI or MT technologies to develop responses is strongly discouraged and if utilised students are to declare where they have used it.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit your practical report through the course Turnitin submission point in Blackboard. Before submitting your assessment item to Turnitin please name your file in the following way. FAMILY NAME_GivenName_StudentID_CourseCode_AssignmentName For example: Bob Smith (ID: 54329876) would name his ABCD1234 essay 1 in the following way. SMITH_Bob_54329876_ABCD1234_Essay1
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: MCQ quizzes on material from lectures
assessments.due_date:
MCQ Quiz 1 15/04/2026 1:30 pm

MCQ Quiz 2 20/05/2026 1:30 pm

The quiz will open 30 min prior to the due times and dates listed.
assessments.task_description:
This assessment will consist of two quizzes with questions covering the lecture material and requiring to choose one correct answer for each question from multiple answers (MCQ). The first part of assessment will be held around mid-semester (week 7) and cover material of lectures from modules 1 to 4. The second part of assessment will be held at the end of the semester (week 12) and cover the material of lectures from modules 5 to 9. Each part will be worth 5%, totalling 10%.

The assessment will require thorough review of the relevant lecture material prior to performing the assessment.  

The assessment will be Blackboard assessment with only one attempt to answer each question.  

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI)and Machine Translation (MT)

This assessment task evaluates students' abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT). Students are advised that the use of AI or MT technologies to develop responses is strongly discouraged and if utilised students are to declare where they have used it.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: Exams submitted after the end of the submission time will incur a late penalty.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The end of semester exam will assess in depth knowledge of material covered during the lectures, the practical components of the course and the ability of students to apply this knowledge. This will be an in-person, closed book, invigilated exam using Inspera. You will need to bring your own device as per instructions below:

my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/information-technology/bring-your-own-device-byod

web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it/learnuq-blackboard-help/inspera-assessment

Further details on the likely format of exam questions will be illustrated by providing practice exam questions and further discussed during the course review lecture. 

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 3(23 Feb - 15 Mar)
learning_activities.topic:
Module 1 - General and applied virology

Prof Watterson will provide welcome to the course lecture, Introduction to wet lab practical and tutorial on preparation of practical report.

Prof Khromykh will deliver a series of lectures on fundamental aspects of Virology including an overview of Virology discipline, virus attachment and entry, viral genome replication and virus maturation and exit. This will be followed by lectures on the development of viral vectors for vaccines and gene therapy applications and advances in molecular manipulations of viral genomes. This module will be concluded by a Q&A workshop.



Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Module 1 - General and applied virology Prof Watterson will provide welcome to the course lecture,  Introduction to wet lab practical and tutorial on preparation of practical report. Prof Khromykh will deliver a series of lectures on fundamental aspects of Virology including an overview of Virology discipline, virus attachment and entry, viral genome replication and virus maturation and exit. This will be followed by lectures on the development of viral vectors for vaccines and gene therapy applications and advances in molecular manipulations of viral genomes. This module will be concluded by a Q&A workshop.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 2 To Week 5(02 Mar - 29 Mar)
learning_activities.topic:
Wet lab practical

Four weeks of practical providing a real laboratory experience for developing practical skills relevant to virus detection, isolation and detection of viral genome, detection of viral proteins and determination of viral titres


Learning outcomes: L07, L09
learning_activities.topic_description: Wet lab practical Four weeks of practical providing a real laboratory experience for developing practical skills relevant to virus detection, isolation and detection of viral genome, detection of viral proteins and determination of viral titres

learning_activities.topic:
Module 2 - Viral diagnostics, epidemiology and evolution

Assoc Prof Hobson-Peters will deliver a block of 3 lectures on viral diagnostics, epidemiology and evolution.


Learning outcomes: L03, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Module 2 - Viral diagnostics, epidemiology and evolution Assoc Prof Hobson-Peters will deliver a block of 3 lectures on viral diagnostics, epidemiology and evolution.

learning_activities.topic:
Module 3 - Structural virology

Prof Watterson will deliver a block of 3 lectures on virus structures and applications of structural knowledge to the development of vaccines and antiviral agents


Learning outcomes: L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Module 3 - Structural virology Prof Watterson will deliver a block of 3 lectures on virus structures and applications of structural knowledge to the development of vaccines and antiviral agents

learning_activities.topic:
Module 4 - Virus-host interactions and Systems Virology

Assoc Prof Andrii Slonchak will deliver a block of 2 lectures on virus-host interactions and system biology of viral infections and Dr Lachlan DeHayr will give tutorial on to computer lab practical.

Learning outcomes: L03, L08
learning_activities.topic_description: Module 4 - Virus-host interactions and Systems Virology Assoc Prof Andrii Slonchak will deliver a block of 2 lectures on virus-host interactions and system biology of viral infections and Dr Lachlan DeHayr will give tutorial on to computer lab practical.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 7 To Week 8(13 Apr - 26 Apr)
learning_activities.topic:
Computer lab practical

Two weeks of practical to learn how to perform computational analysis of global changes in the expression of host genes induced by viral infections and how to interpret the results of this analysis


Learning outcomes: L03, L08, L09
learning_activities.topic_description: Computer lab practical Two weeks of practical to learn how to perform computational analysis of global changes in the expression of host genes induced by viral infections and how to interpret the results of this analysis

learning_activities.topic:
Module 5 - Respiratory viruses

Prof Kirsty Short will present 3 lectures on respiratory viruses - Influenza, SARS-CoV-2 and Respiratory Syncitial Virus
learning_activities.topic_description: Module 5 - Respiratory viruses Prof Kirsty Short will present 3 lectures on respiratory viruses - Influenza, SARS-CoV-2 and Respiratory Syncitial Virus

learning_activities.topic:
Module 6 - Insect viruses

Prof Asgari will deliver a block of 3 lectures on viruses of insects, antiviral defence in insects and insect virus regulation by microRNAs
learning_activities.topic_description: Module 6 - Insect viruses Prof Asgari will deliver a block of 3 lectures on viruses of insects, antiviral defence in insects and insect virus regulation by microRNAs

learning_activities.topic:
Module 7 - Plant viruses

Dr Karl Robinson will deliver a block of 3 lectures on plant viruses, plant virus movement and virus resistance and control in plants.
learning_activities.topic_description: Module 7 - Plant viruses Dr Karl Robinson will deliver a block of 3 lectures on plant viruses, plant virus movement and virus resistance and control in plants.

learning_activities.topic:
Module 8 - Guest lectures - Veterinary viruses

Honorary Assoc Prof Helle Bielefeldt-Ohmann will give two guest lectures on viruses of veterinary importance
learning_activities.topic_description: Module 8 - Guest lectures - Veterinary viruses Honorary Assoc Prof Helle Bielefeldt-Ohmann will give two guest lectures on viruses of veterinary importance

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 11 To Week 12(11 May - 24 May)
learning_activities.topic:
Module 9 - Guest lectures -Diseases caused by viruses

A series of 3 lectures given by guest lecturers on diseases caused by viral infections, including viral oncogenesis (Dr O"Donnell), viral immunodeficiency (Assoc Prof Harrich), viral encephalitis and arthritis (Assoc Prof Rawle)


Learning outcomes: L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Module 9 - Guest lectures -Diseases caused by viruses A series of 3 lectures given by guest lecturers on diseases caused by viral infections, including viral oncogenesis (Dr O"Donnell), viral immunodeficiency (Assoc Prof Harrich), viral encephalitis and arthritis (Assoc Prof Rawle)

learning_activities.topic:
Module 10 - ECR guest lectures

A series of 3 lectures given by Early Career Researcher (ECR) guest lecturers on Research career pathways (Dr Newton), Media Engagement (Dr Isaacs) and Research Commercialization (Dr Parry)

Learning outcomes: L10
learning_activities.topic_description: Module 10 - ECR guest lectures A series of 3 lectures given by Early Career Researcher (ECR) guest lecturers on Research career pathways (Dr Newton), Media Engagement (Dr Isaacs) and Research Commercialization (Dr Parry)
